topic: AI memory systems for LLMs and AI agents — survey papers, benchmarks, and empirical research

findings.claim: AI Meets Brain survey bridges cognitive neuroscience with LLM agent memory, proposing a unified taxonomy across biological memory types (sensory, short-term, long-term with episodic/semantic/procedural subtypes) and artificial memory implementations (contextual, parametric, external storage). Includes security analysis of agent memory attacks/defenses.
findings.evidence: Memory serves as the pivotal nexus bridging past and future... this survey systematically synthesizes interdisciplinary knowledge of memory, connecting insights from cognitive neuroscience with LLM-driven agents.
findings.kai_relevance: Direct philosophical/anatomical grounding for Kai's humanistic memory architecture — justifies bridging human memory models with LLM agent memory design.

findings.claim: Memory in LLM-based Multi-agent Systems (Emory survey) is the first comprehensive review identifying synchronization, access control, scalability, alignment, and safety as distinct research challenges unique to multi-agent memory — not trivial extensions of single-agent memory.
findings.evidence: Memory in LLM-MAS is not a trivial extension of single-agent memory but a distinct research frontier—with new challenges in synchronization, access control, scalability, alignment, and safety.
findings.kai_relevance: Directly informs Kai-1's multi-agent memory topology — must address consistency, access protocols, and shared memory space design.

findings.claim: MemGPT introduces virtual context management via hierarchical memory tiers (main context, working context, archival storage), drawing OS paging inspiration. Enables LLMs to operate beyond context windows. Achieves 35% F1 improvement on document QA exceeding context lengths.
findings.evidence: We propose virtual context management, a technique drawing inspiration from hierarchical memory systems in traditional operating systems that provide the appearance of large memory resources through data movement between fast and slow memory.
findings.kai_relevance: Core reference architecture for Kai-1 external memory — OS-inspired tiered memory directly applicable to Kai's user memory persistence design.

findings.claim: Generative Agents (Stanford) demonstrate LLM-powered agents with memory stream, reflection, and retrieval produce emergent social behaviors (e.g., autonomously organizing a Valentine's party via memory-driven planning, conversation, and coordination across 25 agents).
findings.evidence: Generative agents wake up, cook breakfast, and head to work; artists paint, while authors write; they form opinions, notice each other, and initiate conversations; they remember and reflect on days past as they plan the next day.
findings.kai_relevance: Foundational pattern for Kai's agent memory architecture — memory stream + reflection + retrieval is the baseline for Kai-1 agentic memory design.

findings.claim: Infini-Attention (DeepMind) integrates compressive memory into standard attention, enabling infinite context processing with bounded memory and computation. Combines masked local attention with long-term linear attention in a single Transformer block.
findings.evidence: Infini-attention incorporates a compressive memory into the vanilla attention mechanism and builds in both masked local attention and long-term linear attention mechanisms in a single Transformer block.
findings.kai_relevance: Provides the architectural mechanism for infinite-context memory within the LLM itself — alternative to external RAG for Kai-1 long-context tasks.

findings.claim: LongMemEval benchmarks five core long-term memory abilities (information extraction, multi-session reasoning, temporal reasoning, knowledge updates, abstention) with 500 questions across 115K–1.5M token histories. Long-context LLMs show 30-60% accuracy drop; proposes three-stage memory framework (indexing, retrieval, reading).
findings.evidence: Long-context LLMs show a 30%∼60% performance drop on LongMemEvalS... We then present a unified framework that breaks down the long-term memory design into three stages: indexing, retrieval, and reading.
findings.kai_relevance: Primary benchmark for evaluating Kai-1 memory — covers all critical memory competencies; three-stage framework guides Kai memory system design.

findings.claim: MemoryAgentBench (accepted ICLR 2026) evaluates four core memory competencies (accurate retrieval, test-time learning, long-range understanding, conflict resolution). MemGPT, MIRIX, and RAG agents all fail to master all four; RAG strong on retrieval but weak on global understanding.
findings.evidence: Current methods fall short of mastering all four competencies, underscoring the need for further research into comprehensive memory mechanisms for LLM agents.
findings.kai_relevance: Directly evaluates the type of memory agent Kai-1 will be; identifies specific failure modes (RAG's global understanding gap) that Kai architecture must address.

findings.claim: Terminal-Bench (ICLR 2026) provides 89 hard real-world terminal tasks for evaluating agent memory and execution. Frontier agents resolve <65%. Covers software engineering, ML, security, data science. Not strictly a memory benchmark but tests procedural/episodic memory over long horizons.
findings.evidence: Frontier models and agents score less than 65% on the benchmark... each task features a unique environment, human-written solution, and comprehensive tests for verification.
findings.kai_relevance: Relevant for evaluating Kai-1 procedural memory and long-horizon task execution — complements LongMemEval on the procedural/episodic dimension.

findings.claim: Rethinking Memory Mechanisms of Foundation Agents (multi-institutional survey, Feb 2026) provides unified taxonomy across three dimensions: memory substrate (internal/external), cognitive mechanism (episodic/semantic/sensory/working/procedural), and memory subject (agent/user-centric). Reviews hundreds of papers.
findings.evidence: We provide a unified view of foundation agent memory along three dimensions: memory substrate (internal and external), cognitive mechanism (episodic, semantic, sensory, working, and procedural), and memory subject (agent- and user-centric).
findings.kai_relevance: Most comprehensive recent survey; the three-dimensional taxonomy (substrate × cognitive mechanism × subject) is directly applicable to Kai-1 memory classification and design decisions.

findings.claim: Rethinking Memory in AI: Taxonomy, Operations, Topics, and Future Directions introduces six fundamental memory operations (Consolidation, Updating, Indexing, Forgetting, Retrieval, Compression) and categorizes memory representations into parametric, contextual structured, and contextual unstructured. Analyzed 30K top-tier conference papers (2022-2025) using Relative Citation Index.
findings.evidence: We first categorize memory representations into parametric, contextual structured, and contextual unstructured and then introduce six fundamental memory operations... We collect and review more than 30K top-tier conference papers published between 2022-2025.
findings.kai_relevance: Provides the atomic operations framework for building Kai's memory management layer — the six operations map directly to memory system engineering requirements.

sources.title: MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems
Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
Leave No Context Behind: Efficient Infinite Context Transformers with Infini-attention
LongMemEval: Benchmarking Chat Assistants on Long-Term Interactive Memory
Evaluating Memory in LLM Agents via Incremental Multi-Turn Interactions (MemoryAgentBench)
Terminal-Bench: Benchmarking Agents on Hard, Realistic Tasks in Command Line Interfaces
Memory in LLM-based Multi-agent Systems: Mechanisms, Challenges, and Collective Intelligence
AI Meets Brain: A Unified Survey on Memory Systems from Cognitive Neuroscience to Autonomous Agents
Rethinking Memory Mechanisms of Foundation Agents in the Second Half: A Survey
Rethinking Memory in AI: Taxonomy, Operations, Topics, and Future Directions

gaps: No benchmark specifically evaluates memory for AI safety/alignment in agentic systems — critical gap for Kai-1 safety domain.
Empirical comparisons between MemGPT-style OS memory, RAG, and Infini-attention architectures on practical latency/cost/quality metrics are scarce.
Multi-agent memory consistency protocols remain undefined — no formal theory comparable to database serializability or cache coherence for agent memory.
No standardized evaluation methodology exists for measuring the effect of memory on agent alignment and value consistency across sessions.
MemoryAgentBench and LongMemEval use different competency taxonomies — the field needs a unified evaluation standard before meaningful cross-system comparison.
Lack of benchmarks for Russian-language memory agents — Kai-1 operates in a Russian-first context, but all major benchmarks are English-only.
